meta.title: Contract Law Knowledge Base
meta.description: Structured representation of contract law doctrine — portable across projects. Built from Oranburg, Contract Law: Rules, Cases, and Problems (2d ed., CAP 2025), plus primary sources (R2d, UCC, case law).

chapters.title: What Is Contract Law?
keyRules: Contract law is the law of promises — voluntary, private law
Law vs. equity: legal remedies (damages) vs. equitable remedies (specific performance, injunctions, rescission)
R2d is persuasive authority; UCC is binding law for goods
UCC Article 2 governs sale of goods; predominant purpose test for hybrids
Four justifications: efficiency, reliance, fairness, autonomy
Objective standard: external manifestations, not secret intent
chapters.keyQuotation: Contract law is the law of promises.

chapters.title: What Is a Contract?
keyRules: R2d § 1: Contract = promise(s) for which law gives remedy or recognizes as duty
R2d § 2(1): Promise = manifestation of intention to act, justifying promisee in understanding commitment made
R2d § 3: Agreement = mutual assent; Bargain = agreement to exchange
Statement of intention ≠ promise (Pappas v. Bever: 'I intend to' is non-binding)
Bilateral = promise for promise; Unilateral = promise for performance
Non-bargain contracts enforced through equity (promissory estoppel)
Objective theory: 'meeting of the minds' is outdated — overt acts control (Steinberg)

cases.name: Steinberg v. Chicago Medical School
cases.rule: Contract formed through sequence of acts; secret intent immaterial; objective manifestation suffices

cases.name: Pappas v. Bever
cases.rule: 'I intend to subscribe' is statement of intention, not promise; partial payments don't cure language defect

chapters.keyQuotation: Contrary to popular belief, contract law does not require a 'meeting of the minds.'

chapters.title: Bargains
keyRules: R2d § 17(1): Contract requires bargain with mutual assent + consideration
R2d § 18: Mutual assent requires each party make promise or begin/render performance
R2d § 19(1): Assent may be by words, acts, or failure to act
Objective theory: reasonable person standard, not secret intent
R2d § 20: Misunderstanding — no assent if materially different meanings and neither knows other's meaning
Joking is no defense if other party reasonably believes offer serious (Lucy v. Zehmer)

cases.name: Lucy v. Zehmer
cases.rule: Objective manifestation controls; joking no defense when reasonable person would believe agreement was serious; specific performance awarded for farm sale written on guest check

cases.name: Raffles v. Wichelhaus (Peerless)
cases.rule: No mutual assent when parties attach materially different meanings to same term (two ships named Peerless); neither knew other's meaning

chapters.keyQuotation: We must look to the outward expression of a person as manifesting his intention rather than to his secret and unexpressed intention.

chapters.title: Offers
keyRules: R2d § 24: Offer = manifestation of willingness to enter bargain, justifying offeree in understanding acceptance will conclude it
R2d § 26: Preliminary negotiations are not offers — person doesn't intend to be bound without further manifestation
R2d § 33: Terms must be reasonably certain — basis for determining breach and remedy
Essential terms (common law): parties, subject matter, quantity, price
UCC § 2-305: Price term can be left open for goods if market price determinable
Advertisements generally NOT offers (R2d § 26 cmt. b) — exception: specific item + price + quantity + manner of acceptance (Lefkowitz)

cases.name: Lefkowitz v. Great Minneapolis Surplus Store
cases.rule: Ad was offer because it specified item, price ($1), quantity (1 stole), and acceptance method ('first come first served'); store's 'house rule' excluding men was improper post-hoc limitation

cases.name: Leonard v. Pepsico
cases.rule: Harrier jet ad was not offer — no reasonable person would believe a $23M military jet was offered for $700K in Pepsi Points; humor/absurdity negates objective intent

chapters.keyQuotation: An offer is a proposal to enter a voluntary bargain or exchange.

chapters.title: Termination of the Offer
keyRules: R2d § 36(1): Four methods: (a) rejection/counteroffer, (b) lapse, (c) revocation, (d) death/incapacity
R2d § 38: Rejection terminates unless offeror manifests contrary intention
R2d § 39: Counteroffer = substitute bargain relating to same matter; terminates original
R2d § 42: Revocation effective when offeree receives manifestation of unwillingness
R2d § 43: Constructive revocation — definite inconsistent action + reliable information
R2d § 41: Lapse after specified time or reasonable time; conversation rule
R2d § 48: Death/incapacity terminates automatically without notice
OFUR irrevocability: Option contracts (R2d § 25), Firm offers (UCC § 2-205, merchants, 3 months max), Unilateral part-performance (R2d § 45), Reliance (R2d § 87(2))

cases.name: Smaligo v. Fireman's Fund Insurance Co.
cases.rule: Filing for arbitration after settlement offer = indirect rejection through conduct inconsistent with acceptance

chapters.keyQuotation: Offers are ephemeral. They quickly mature into contracts or decay into nothing.

chapters.title: Acceptance
keyRules: R2d § 50(1): Acceptance = manifestation of assent to offer's terms in manner invited/required
R2d § 58: Acceptance must comply with offer's requirements (mirror image rule)
R2d § 59: Conditional acceptance = counteroffer, not acceptance
R2d § 61: Requesting change ≠ conditioning acceptance (request OK, condition kills it)
UCC § 2-207(1): Definite seasonable acceptance operates as acceptance even with additional/different terms, UNLESS expressly conditional
UCC § 2-207(2): Additional terms between merchants become part unless: offer limits, materially alters, or offeror objects
Knock-out rule (majority): conflicting 'different terms' cancel; replaced by UCC gap fillers
R2d § 63(a): Mailbox rule — acceptance effective on dispatch, not receipt
Mailbox rule exceptions: option contracts (receipt), near-instantaneous communication, rejection sent first, estoppel

cases.name: State DOT v. Providence & Worcester Railroad
cases.rule: Immaterial differences don't invalidate acceptance; changing buyer name and relieving track removal obligation were not additional terms

cases.name: Flender Corp. v. Tippins International
cases.rule: Conflicting forum clauses knocked out under UCC § 2-207; neither Vienna nor Chicago prevails; default jurisdiction applies

chapters.keyQuotation: The mere addition of a collateral or immaterial matter will not prevent the formation of a contract.

chapters.title: Consideration
keyRules: R2d § 71(1): Consideration requires bargained-for performance or return promise
R2d § 71(2): Bargained for = sought by promisor in exchange, given by promisee in exchange
R2d § 71(3): Performance may be act, forbearance, or creation/modification/destruction of legal relation
R2d § 79: No requirement of equivalence — adequacy not examined (peppercorn doctrine)
Benefit/detriment test (historical): benefit to promisor OR detriment to promisee
Modern bargained-for exchange test: promise must induce detriment AND detriment must induce promise (Holmes formula)
Gratuitous promises not enforceable; conditional gifts not consideration unless condition benefits promisor

cases.name: Hamer v. Sidway
cases.rule: Forbearance from legal right (drinking, smoking, gambling) = valid consideration regardless of whether promisor benefited; nephew's restriction of lawful freedom suffices

cases.name: Pennsy Supply v. American Ash Recycling
cases.rule: Free hazardous waste removal = consideration because promisor avoided disposal costs; applies to 'bads' not just goods; conditional gift vs. bargain distinguished

chapters.keyQuotation: Consideration is the glue that makes enforceable promises meaningful and ensures mutual benefit.

chapters.title: Promissory Estoppel
keyRules: R2d § 90(1): Promise binding without consideration if: (1) promisor should reasonably expect to induce action/forbearance, (2) promise does induce such action/forbearance, (3) injustice avoidable only by enforcement
Four elements: clear promise, reasonable reliance, detriment, injustice
Remedy may be limited as justice requires (not always full expectation damages)
Promise must be clear and definite from reasonable person's perspective
Reliance must be actual, reasonable, and foreseeable to promisor
Detriment: promisee worse off than if promise never made (out-of-pocket or opportunity cost)

cases.name: Ricketts v. Scothorn
cases.rule: Grandfather's promissory note induced granddaughter to quit job; estoppel bars raising lack of consideration; voluntary conduct inducing change of position for the worse

cases.name: Conrad v. Fields
cases.rule: Wealthy friend promised to pay law school tuition; plaintiff quit $45K job in reliance; full tuition damages ($87,314) awarded; clear promise + foreseeable reliance + substantial detriment + injustice

chapters.keyQuotation: Promissory estoppel serves as a safety net within contract law, filling the gaps left by doctrines like consideration.

chapters.title: Promissory Restitution
keyRules: R2d § 86(1): Promise in recognition of benefit previously received is binding to extent necessary to prevent injustice
General rule (Mills v. Wyman): moral obligation alone insufficient — past consideration is no consideration
Exception: material benefit + subsequent promise = enforceable (Webb v. McGowin)
Five exception categories: benefit received, mistake corrected, emergency services, past business gift, unpaid contract revived
Distinguishes from estoppel: benefit precedes promise (backward-looking) vs. reliance follows promise (forward-looking)

cases.name: Mills v. Wyman
cases.rule: Father's promise to reimburse stranger who cared for adult son unenforceable — no material benefit to promisor, pure moral obligation insufficient

cases.name: Webb v. McGowin
cases.rule: Employee saved employer's life at great personal injury; employer's subsequent promise to pay $15 biweekly enforceable — life has material pecuniary value; moral obligation + material benefit = sufficient

cases.name: Drake v. Bell
cases.rule: Contractor mistakenly repaired wrong house; homeowner's promise to pay enforceable — material benefit conferred under expectation of payment

chapters.keyQuotation: A moral obligation is sufficient consideration to support a subsequent promise where the promisor has received a material benefit.

chapters.title: The Statute of Frauds
keyRules: R2d § 110: MYLEGS categories requiring signed writing — Marriage, Year-long, Land, Executorship, Goods $500+, Suretyship
R2d § 131: Memorandum must identify subject matter, indicate contract exists, state essential terms, signed by party to be charged
R2d § 132: Multiple writings may be combined
R2d § 134: Signature = any symbol made with intent to authenticate
R2d § 139: Promissory estoppel exception — reliance substantial, foreseeable, other remedies unavailable
R2d § 129: Part performance exception for land
UCC § 2-201: Merchant confirmatory memo, special manufacture, admission, goods/payment accepted exceptions

cases.name: McIntosh v. Murphy
cases.rule: Oral one-year employment contract enforceable under promissory estoppel where employee relocated 2,200 miles in reliance

cases.name: Sterling v. Taylor
cases.rule: Extrinsic evidence may clarify ambiguous memorandum terms but cannot prove terms at odds with writing

chapters.keyQuotation: The statute of frauds is not just a technical hurdle but a reminder of contract law's dual role: clarity while accommodating human realities.

chapters.title: Mistake
keyRules: R2d § 152: Mutual mistake — voidable when both parties mistaken as to basic assumption, material effect, neither bore risk
R2d § 153: Unilateral mistake — requires unconscionability OR other party had reason to know/caused mistake
R2d § 154: Risk allocation — three Cs: Contract (allocated by terms), Conscious ignorance (aware of limited knowledge), Court (reasonable allocation)
Mistake of identity/nature (voidable) vs. mistake of value/quality (generally not voidable)

cases.name: Wood v. Boynton
cases.rule: Selling uncut diamond for $1 believing it topaz — no mutual mistake; parties transacted over 'stone of uncertain value'; seller assumed risk

cases.name: Sherwood v. Walker
cases.rule: Mutual mistake that cow was barren (actually fertile) goes to substance/nature, not mere quality; contract voidable

cases.name: DePrince v. Starboard Cruise
cases.rule: Jewelry manager quoted per-carat price as total — unilateral mistake defense succeeds where buyer's knowledge/silence induced mistake

chapters.keyQuotation: The doctrine of mistake involves a careful balance between fairness and efficiency.

chapters.title: Improper Bargaining
keyRules: R2d § 159: Misrepresentation = assertion not in accord with facts
R2d § 162: Fraudulent misrepresentation = false assertion + scienter + intent to induce + inducement + justifiable reliance
R2d § 160-161: Concealment and duty to disclose (fiduciary, correct prior statements, basic assumption mistakes)
R2d § 175: Duress = improper threat precluding reasonable alternative
R2d § 177: Undue influence = unfair persuasion in relation of trust/confidence
R2d § 208: Unconscionability = procedural (bargaining process) + substantive (terms)

cases.name: Quebodeaux v. Quebodeaux
cases.rule: Wife signed separation agreement under duress — husband threatened to take children and declare her unfit; three elements: involuntary, no reasonable alternative, other party caused circumstances

chapters.keyQuotation: Contract law doesn't punish mere advantage, but it does intervene when the structure of a deal becomes distorted by withheld truths that matter.

chapters.title: Incapacity
keyRules: R2d § 12: No capacity = no binding contract; full capacity unless: guardianship, infant, mental illness, intoxication
R2d § 14: Infant (under 18) contracts are voidable; can disaffirm within reasonable time after majority; exception for necessaries
R2d § 15(1)(a): Mental illness — unable to understand nature and consequences
R2d § 15(1)(b): Mental illness — unable to act reasonably where other party should know of condition
R2d § 16: Intoxication — other party must have reason to know of inability to understand

cases.name: Webster St. Partnership v. Sheridan
cases.rule: Teenager's lease voidable despite continued residence after turning 19; timely disaffirmance; apartment not a 'necessary' when could live at home

chapters.keyQuotation: The incapacity doctrines share a common purpose of ensuring that contracts are founded on voluntary, informed consent.

chapters.title: Ambiguity
keyRules: Patent ambiguity: evident on face of contract; Latent ambiguity: apparent only when applied to circumstances
Hierarchy of evidence: plain meaning → specific terms → structure → course of performance → course of dealing → trade usage → parol evidence
Common law: restrictive — requires intrinsic ambiguity before admitting extrinsic evidence
UCC: broader — permits extrinsic evidence even if writing appears complete

cases.name: Frigaliment Importing Co. v. B.N.S. International
cases.rule: What is 'chicken'? Multi-source interpretation approach — dictionaries, trade usage, negotiations, price, industry practice; buyer failed burden to prove 'chicken' meant only young frying chickens

chapters.keyQuotation: The making of a contract depends not on the agreement of two minds in one intention, but on the agreement of two sets of external signs.

chapters.title: Intrinsic Evidence
keyRules: Semantic canons: plain meaning, technical meaning, noscitur a sociis, ejusdem generis, last antecedent, whole agreement, avoid surplusage, consistent usage, specific over general, negotiated over preprinted
Policy canons: good faith (R2d § 205; UCC § 1-304), contra proferentem (R2d § 206), public policy (R2d § 207), unconscionability (R2d § 208)

cases.name: In re Motors Liquidation Co.
cases.rule: Death was consequence of prior accident, not separate 'incident'; noscitur a sociis trumps surplusage canon; 'accidents' and 'incidents' given related meaning

chapters.keyQuotation: Noscitur a sociis — a word is known by the company it keeps.

chapters.title: Extrinsic Evidence
keyRules: Course of performance (UCC § 1-303(a)): repeated conduct between parties to THIS transaction
Course of dealing (R2d § 223; UCC § 1-303(b)): conduct from PRIOR transactions between same parties
Usage of trade (UCC § 1-303(c); R2d § 222): industry practice with regularity of observance
Implied-in-fact terms: arise from circumstances, require proving intent
Implied-in-law terms: imposed by courts for fairness, no intent required

cases.name: Wood v. Lucy, Lady Duff-Gordon
cases.rule: Exclusive agency agreement implied promise to use reasonable efforts; Cardozo: 'the whole writing may be instinct with an obligation, imperfectly expressed'

cases.name: Nanakuli Paving v. Shell Oil
cases.rule: Trade usage of price protection in asphalt industry supplemented written contract; course of dealing showed Shell had protected Nanakuli twice before

chapters.keyQuotation: A promise may be lacking, and yet the whole writing may be 'instinct with an obligation, imperfectly expressed.'

chapters.title: The Parol Evidence Rule
keyRules: R2d § 213: Integrated agreement discharges prior agreements to extent inconsistent
R2d § 209: Integration = writing constituting final expression of one or more terms
R2d § 210: Complete integration = final AND exclusive; Partial = final but not exclusive
R2d § 216: Not completely integrated if omits consistent term (a) agreed for separate consideration or (b) naturally omitted
UCC § 2-202: Written terms not contradicted by prior agreement; supplementable by course of dealing/usage/performance
Exceptions: fraud/duress/mistake, ambiguity, collateral agreements (independent + consistent + naturally omitted), conditions precedent (R2d § 217)
Merger clauses: usually dispositive but debated (Williston: conclusive; Corbin: rebuttable)

cases.name: Gianni v. R. Russell & Co.
cases.rule: Written lease was complete integration; oral promise of soda exclusivity barred — within scope of written agreement covering permitted/prohibited uses

cases.name: UAW-GM v. KSL Recreation
cases.rule: Merger clause conclusive on integration question; parol evidence of union-staff promise barred despite collateral agreement argument

chapters.keyQuotation: The writing cannot prove its own completeness and accuracy. (Corbin, dissent position)

chapters.title: Warranties
keyRules: UCC § 2-313(1): Express warranty — affirmation of fact/promise, description, or sample that is part of basis of bargain; no magic words required
UCC § 2-314: Implied merchantability — merchant seller; goods fit for ordinary purposes, fair average quality, adequately packaged
UCC § 2-315: Implied fitness for particular purpose — seller knows buyer's purpose + knows buyer relies on seller's judgment + buyer actually relies
UCC § 2-316: Disclaimers — merchantability must mention word and be conspicuous; fitness must be written and conspicuous; 'as is' can exclude all implied
UCC § 2-714: Damages = difference between value as promised and value as delivered
Puffery/opinion ≠ warranty; must be definite factual assertion

cases.name: Daughtrey v. Ashe
cases.rule: Appraisal form stating 'H color and v.v.s. quality' was express warranty — description became part of basis of bargain; no buyer reliance required under UCC

cases.name: Carlson v. General Motors
cases.rule: Unconscionability of implied warranty limitation requires factual hearing; seller's knowledge of inherent defects relevant beyond mere reasonableness

chapters.keyQuotation: The 'part of the basis of the bargain' language does not establish a buyer's reliance requirement.

chapters.title: Conditions
keyRules: R2d § 224: Condition = event, not certain to occur, that must occur before performance becomes due
R2d § 225(3): Non-occurrence of condition is NOT breach unless party promised condition would occur
R2d § 227: Doubts resolved to reduce risk of forfeiture
R2d § 229: Court may excuse non-occurrence if disproportionate forfeiture
Condition precedent: must occur BEFORE duty arises; Condition subsequent: discharges duty that already arose
R2d § 234: Concurrent conditions — simultaneous performance required
Express conditions: strictly construed; Implied conditions: satisfied by substantial performance
R2d § 228: Satisfaction conditions — objective standard preferred; subjective only for personal aesthetics

cases.name: Kingston v. Preston
cases.rule: Lord Mansfield: three types of covenants (mutual independent, dependent conditions, concurrent); security requirement was condition precedent to seller's duty

cases.name: Morrison v. Bare
cases.rule: Condition ≠ promise; failure of condition discharges duty but is NOT breach; buyer had no right to demand lower price when furnace repair condition failed

chapters.keyQuotation: A condition is a fact or an event and is not an expression of intention or an assurance. A promise creates a legal duty; a condition creates no right or duty.

chapters.title: Substantial Performance and Material Breach
keyRules: R2d § 241: Five factors for materiality — (a) extent of deprivation, (b) adequacy of compensation, (c) extent of part performance, (d) likelihood of cure, (e) good faith
Substantial performance: implied conditions satisfied by less-than-perfect performance if deviation is trivial and innocent
Willful transgressor must accept penalty; innocent transgressor may hope for mercy
UCC § 2-601: Perfect tender rule for goods — buyer may reject if goods fail in ANY respect
UCC § 2-508: Seller's right to cure within contract time or reasonable time
Damages for non-material breach: difference in value, not cost of replacement

cases.name: Jacob & Youngs v. Kent
cases.rule: Wrong pipe brand in mansion = trivial innocent omission; substantial performance doctrine applies; damages = difference in value (nominal), not cost of replacement (demolition); Cardozo: 'the willful transgressor must accept the penalty of his transgression'

chapters.keyQuotation: The courts never say that one who makes a contract fills the measure of his duty by less than full performance. They do say, however, that an omission, both trivial and innocent, will sometimes be atoned for by allowance of the resulting damage.

chapters.title: Repudiation
keyRules: R2d § 250(a): Repudiation = unequivocal statement indicating total breach
R2d § 250(b): Voluntary act rendering obligor unable/apparently unable to perform
R2d § 251: Demand for adequate assurance when reasonable grounds for insecurity
UCC § 2-609: Written demand for adequate assurance; failure to respond within 30 days = repudiation
UCC § 2-610: Aggrieved party may suspend performance, seek cover, or sue immediately
Anticipatory repudiation: breach BEFORE time for performance arrives

cases.name: Hochster v. De La Tour
cases.rule: Courier could sue immediately when employer repudiated before start date; established anticipatory repudiation doctrine

chapters.keyQuotation: A statement is a repudiation if a party unequivocally refuses to perform.

chapters.title: Excuse
keyRules: R2d § 261: Impossibility — supervening event makes performance impossible; duty discharged unless party bore risk
R2d § 266: Impracticability — extreme difficulty/cost may discharge (high bar — not mere economic hardship)

R2d § 265: Frustration of purpose — performance possible but underlying purpose destroyed; discharged if: (1) principal purpose substantially frustrated, (2) non-occurrence was basic assumption, (3) not party's fault
UCC § 2-615: Commercial impracticability for goods — performance impracticable by contingency non-occurrence of which was basic assumption

cases.name: Taylor v. Caldwell
cases.rule: Music hall destroyed by fire; both parties excused; continued existence of essential thing is implied condition

cases.name: Krell v. Henry
cases.rule: Coronation postponed; room rental purpose frustrated; three-part test: foundation of contract, performance prevented, event unforeseeable

cases.name: Transatlantic Financing v. United States
cases.rule: Suez Canal closure increased shipping cost ~14%; not impracticable — must be unconscionable burden, not mere economic hardship

chapters.keyQuotation: The parties contracted on the basis of the continued existence of the Music Hall...that being essential to their performance.

chapters.title: Modification and Discharge
keyRules: R2d § 73: Pre-existing duty rule — performing existing obligation is NOT consideration for modification
R2d § 89(a): Modification binding if fair and equitable in view of unanticipated circumstances
UCC § 2-209(1): Modification needs NO consideration — only good faith required
R2d § 279: Substituted contract replaces original entirely
R2d § 281: Accord = agreement to accept different performance; satisfaction = execution discharges duty
R2d § 317-318: Assignment (transfers rights) and delegation (transfers duties) — personal services not delegable

cases.name: Alaska Packers v. Domenico
cases.rule: Sailors demanding double pay mid-season; performing pre-existing duty = no consideration; modification unenforceable

cases.name: Angel v. Murray
cases.rule: Garbage collector's mid-contract increase upheld — fair and equitable given unexpected population surge; modern relaxation of pre-existing duty rule per R2d § 89

chapters.keyQuotation: A promise modifying a duty under a contract not fully performed on either side is binding if the modification is fair and equitable in view of circumstances not anticipated by the parties.

chapters.title: Money Damages for Nonperformance
keyRules: R2d § 347: Expectation damages = position party would have been in had contract been performed
R2d § 351: Foreseeability limitation (Hadley v. Baxendale) — (a) naturally arising OR (b) in contemplation of both parties
R2d § 352: Certainty — damages not recoverable if speculative
R2d § 350: Mitigation — injured party must use reasonable efforts to avoid loss
Direct damages (natural consequence) vs. consequential damages (special circumstances, must be foreseeable)
Incidental damages: costs of arranging substitute performance

cases.name: Hadley v. Baxendale
cases.rule: Lost mill profits not recoverable because carrier didn't know mill would be idle; damages limited to (a) naturally arising or (b) in parties' contemplation at contracting; foundational foreseeability rule

chapters.keyQuotation: The damages which the other party ought to receive should be such as may fairly and reasonably be considered either arising naturally from such breach, or such as may reasonably be supposed to have been in the contemplation of both parties at the time they made the contract.

chapters.title: Money Damages for Defective Performance
keyRules: R2d § 348: Cost of repair when proportionate; diminished value when cost grossly disproportionate to loss
Economic waste doctrine: no demolition for trivial defect when value difference is minimal
Owner entitled to completion cost UNLESS grossly and unfairly out of proportion to good attained

cases.name: Peevyhouse v. Garland Coal & Mining
cases.rule: Strip mine restoration cost $29K but property value increase minimal; damages = diminished value, not cost of completion; proportionality principle

chapters.keyQuotation: The owner is entitled to the money which will permit him to complete, unless the cost of completion is grossly and unfairly out of proportion to the good to be attained.

chapters.title: Limitations on Money Damages
keyRules: Three limitations apply to ALL damages (expectation, reliance, restitution):
1. Foreseeability (R2d § 351): damages beyond reasonable contemplation excluded
2. Certainty (R2d § 352): speculative/uncertain damages excluded; must prove with reasonable certainty
3. Mitigation (R2d § 350): injured party must take reasonable steps to minimize loss; failure reduces recovery
chapters.keyQuotation: All damages — expectation, reliance, restitution — are subject to foreseeability, certainty, and mitigation limitations.

chapters.title: Alternative Remedies
keyRules: R2d § 357: Specific performance when money damages inadequate (unique goods, land, irreplaceable)
R2d § 376: Restitution — return value conferred to prevent unjust enrichment
R2d § 371: Rescission — cancel contract and restore parties to pre-contract position
Equitable remedies are exceptional — courts prefer legal remedies and resort to equity only when necessary
Injunctions: prevent actions causing irreparable harm
chapters.keyQuotation: Equitable remedies remain exceptional — courts prefer legal remedies and resort to equity only when justice requires.

chapters.title: Third-Party Beneficiaries
keyRules: R2d § 302(1): Intended beneficiary — recognition of enforceable right appropriate to effectuate parties' intent; EITHER (a) performance satisfies promisee's obligation to pay beneficiary OR (b) circumstances show promisee intends benefit
R2d § 302(2): Incidental beneficiary — not intended; NO enforceable rights
R2d § 311: Promisor and promisee can modify/rescind before beneficiary's rights vest
R2d § 309: Vesting occurs when beneficiary materially changes position, brings suit, or manifests assent
Creditor beneficiary: satisfying pre-existing debt; Donee beneficiary: gift intent

cases.name: Sovereign Bank v. BJ's Wholesale
cases.rule: Card issuer was incidental beneficiary of merchant agreement — Visa Operating Regulations protected all members but parties' intent focused on Visa's internal enforcement, not individual issuer suits

chapters.keyQuotation: Incidental beneficiaries never have enforceable rights even if they receive some benefit, unless contracting parties expressly intended to grant them enforceable rights.

restatement.title: Contract Defined
restatement.text: A contract is a promise or a set of promises for the breach of which the law gives a remedy, or the performance of which the law in some way recognizes as a duty.

restatement.title: Promise Defined
restatement.text: A promise is a manifestation of intention to act or refrain from acting in a specified way, so made as to justify a promisee in understanding that a commitment has been made.

restatement.title: Agreement; Bargain
restatement.text: An agreement is a manifestation of mutual assent on the part of two or more persons. A bargain is an agreement to exchange promises or to exchange a promise for a performance or to exchange performances.

restatement.title: Requirement of a Bargain
restatement.text: The formation of a contract requires a bargain in which there is a manifestation of mutual assent to the exchange and a consideration.

restatement.title: Misunderstanding
restatement.text: No mutual assent if parties attach materially different meanings and neither knows or has reason to know the other's meaning.

restatement.title: Offer Defined
restatement.text: An offer is the manifestation of willingness to enter into a bargain, so made as to justify another person in understanding that his assent to that bargain is invited and will conclude it.

restatement.title: Termination Methods
restatement.text: Offeree's power terminated by: (a) rejection/counteroffer, (b) lapse, (c) revocation, (d) death/incapacity.

restatement.title: Part Performance on Unilateral
restatement.text: Where offer invites acceptance by performance, option contract created when offeree begins performance.

restatement.title: Acceptance Defined
restatement.text: Acceptance of an offer is a manifestation of assent to the terms thereof made by the offeree in a manner invited or required by the offer.

restatement.title: Mailbox Rule
restatement.text: Acceptance effective upon dispatch unless offer provides otherwise.

restatement.title: Consideration
restatement.text: To constitute consideration, a performance or return promise must be bargained for. Bargained for if sought by promisor in exchange and given by promisee in exchange.

restatement.title: Adequacy
restatement.text: If the requirement of consideration is met, there is no additional requirement of equivalence in the values exchanged.

restatement.title: Promissory Restitution
restatement.text: A promise made in recognition of a benefit previously received by the promisor from the promisee is binding to the extent necessary to prevent injustice.

restatement.title: Promissory Estoppel
restatement.text: A promise which the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance and which does induce such action or forbearance is binding if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement.

restatement.title: Statute of Frauds Classes
restatement.text: MYLEGS: Marriage, Year-long, Land, Executorship, Goods $500+, Suretyship.

restatement.title: Mutual Mistake
restatement.text: Contract voidable when both parties mistaken as to basic assumption with material effect and adversely affected party did not bear risk.

restatement.title: Undue Influence
restatement.text: Unfair persuasion of party under domination or justified in assuming other will act in their welfare.

restatement.title: Parol Evidence Rule
restatement.text: Binding integrated agreement discharges prior agreements to extent inconsistent.

restatement.title: Condition Defined
restatement.text: An event, not certain to occur, which must occur before performance becomes due.

restatement.title: Material Breach Factors
restatement.text: Five factors: extent of deprivation, adequacy of compensation, extent of part performance, likelihood of cure, good faith.

restatement.title: Repudiation
restatement.text: Statement indicating obligor will commit total breach, or voluntary act rendering unable to perform.

restatement.title: Frustration of Purpose
restatement.text: Where principal purpose substantially frustrated by event not party's fault whose non-occurrence was basic assumption, duties discharged.

restatement.title: Third-Party Beneficiaries
restatement.text: Intended beneficiary if recognition of enforceable right appropriate to effectuate parties' intent.

restatement.title: Expectation Damages
restatement.text: Damages put injured party in position they would have been in had contract been performed.

ucc.title: Scope
ucc.text: Article 2 applies to transactions in goods.

ucc.title: Statute of Frauds for Goods
ucc.text: Contracts for sale of goods $500+ require writing; exceptions: merchant memo, special manufacture, admission, acceptance.

ucc.title: Firm Offers
ucc.text: Merchant's signed writing giving assurance of irrevocability; not revocable for stated time or reasonable time, max 3 months.

ucc.title: Battle of the Forms
ucc.text: Definite seasonable acceptance operates as acceptance even with additional/different terms unless expressly conditional.

ucc.title: Modification
ucc.text: Agreement modifying contract needs no consideration to be binding.

ucc.title: Express Warranties
ucc.text: Created by affirmation of fact, description, or sample that becomes part of basis of bargain.

ucc.title: Implied Merchantability
ucc.text: Merchant sellers warrant goods are fit for ordinary purposes.

ucc.title: Implied Fitness
ucc.text: When seller knows buyer's particular purpose and buyer relies on seller's judgment.

ucc.title: Perfect Tender
ucc.text: Buyer may reject if goods fail in any respect to conform to contract.

ucc.title: Adequate Assurance
ucc.text: Party may demand adequate assurance of performance in writing when reasonable grounds for insecurity.

ucc.title: Commercial Impracticability
ucc.text: Delay or non-delivery excused if performance impracticable by contingency whose non-occurrence was basic assumption.

enrichment._note: Supplementary materials linked by the professor. Each item references doctrines and chapters.
